{{Short description|Einstein's theory of gravitation describing spacetime curvature}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = General relativity
| image = GPB circling earth.jpg
| caption = Artist impression of [[Gravity Probe B]] measuring spacetime curvature around Earth.
| topics = [[Gravitation]], [[Spacetime]], [[Special relativity]], [[Cosmology]]
| key_equation = [[Einstein field equations]]
}}

'''General relativity''' (GR), also known as the '''general theory of relativity''', is the geometric [[theory]] of [[gravitation]] published by [[Albert Einstein]] in 1915. It is the current description of gravitation in modern physics. General relativity generalises [[special relativity]] and refines [[Newton's law of universal gravitation]], providing a unified description of gravity as a geometric property of [[spacetime]].

The central idea of general relativity is that [[mass]] and [[energy]] curve the geometry of spacetime, and this curvature affects the motion of [[matter]] and [[light]]. As [[John Archibald Wheeler]] succinctly put it: "Spacetime tells matter how to move; matter tells spacetime how to curve."

== Einstein field equations ==
{{Main|Einstein field equations}}
The relationship between matter and spacetime geometry is given by:
:<math>R_{\mu\nu} - \frac{1}{2}R g_{\mu\nu} + \Lambda g_{\mu\nu} = \frac{8\pi G}{c^4} T_{\mu\nu}</math>

The left-hand side describes the geometry of spacetime (the [[Einstein tensor]] <math>G_{\mu\nu}</math>). The right-hand side describes the distribution of matter and energy (the [[stress–energy tensor]] <math>T_{\mu\nu}</math>). The [[cosmological constant]] <math>\Lambda</math> was originally introduced by Einstein to achieve a static universe.

== Principles ==

=== Equivalence principle ===
The [[equivalence principle]] states that the effects of gravity are locally indistinguishable from acceleration. In a small freely falling reference frame, the laws of physics reduce to those of special relativity.

=== Geodesic motion ===
Free particles follow [[geodesic]]s—the straightest possible paths in curved spacetime:
:<math>\frac{d^2 x^\mu}{d\tau^2} + \Gamma^\mu_{\alpha\beta}\frac{dx^\alpha}{d\tau}\frac{dx^\beta}{d\tau} = 0</math>
where <math>\Gamma^\mu_{\alpha\beta}</math> are the [[Christoffel symbols]] and <math>\tau</math> is the [[proper time]].

== Predictions and tests ==

=== Classical tests ===
# '''Perihelion precession of Mercury''' — 43 arcseconds per century, unexplained by Newtonian gravity
# '''Deflection of light by the Sun''' — confirmed by the 1919 [[solar eclipse]] expedition led by [[Arthur Eddington]]
# '''Gravitational redshift''' — light loses energy climbing out of a gravitational well

=== Modern tests ===
* [[Gravitational time dilation]] — verified in [[GPS]] satellites
* [[Gravitational wave]]s — directly detected by [[LIGO]] in 2015 from a binary black hole merger
* [[Frame-dragging]] — confirmed by [[Gravity Probe B]]
* [[Black hole]] imaging — [[Event Horizon Telescope]], 2019

== Black holes ==
{{Main|Black hole}}
One of the most dramatic predictions of general relativity is the existence of [[black hole]]s—regions of spacetime where gravity is so strong that nothing, not even light, can escape. The boundary is the [[event horizon]]. The [[Schwarzschild metric]] describes a non-rotating black hole:
:<math>ds^2 = -\left(1 - \frac{2GM}{c^2 r}\right)c^2 dt^2 + \left(1 - \frac{2GM}{c^2 r}\right)^{-1} dr^2 + r^2 d\Omega^2</math>

== Cosmology ==
{{Main|Physical cosmology}}
General relativity is the theoretical foundation for modern [[cosmology]]. The [[Friedmann–Lemaître–Robertson–Walker metric]] (FLRW metric) describes a homogeneous, isotropic expanding universe and leads to the [[Big Bang]] model.

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Black hole]]
* [[Cosmological constant]]
* [[Cosmology]]
* [[Gravitation]]
* [[Gravitational wave]]
* [[Spacetime]]
* [[Special relativity]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:General relativity| ]]
[[Category:Theories of gravity]]
[[Category:Albert Einstein]]
[[Category:Fundamental physics concepts]]
